summary: Stancil argues that corporate values are inherently vague and often serve as flexible, quasi-legal tools that can be weaponized by management or disputed by employees. The real issue isn’t that companies want values, but that these slogans obscure tradeoffs between profit and principle, creating ambiguity and conflict. He proposes replacing abstract values with concrete precedents that show how a company actually behaves under pressure.
money_quotes: Most for-profit companies have two sets of values.
Values often being framed as constitutional beliefs, they are actually squishy gestures at unknown and unknowable tradeoffs.
Only the former give both executives and employees the ability to pick and choose when and how to apply a rule to their liking.
Rather than making people deduce specific behaviors from a set of generalized values, give them the precedents and let them induce the principles for themselves.
People can say they will do anything; but nobody can escape what they’ve done.
key_insight: Corporate values are less useful as slogans than as documented precedents that reveal how a company actually balances profit and principle in real situations.
